structure.explanation: The presentation followed the basic structure of the slides, but the narration did not show clear transitions.
structure.calculation: Three distinct slide titles provided a basic frame; no explicit three-point frame was detected.

fluency.explanation: The speech had no filler words but also showed no conversational markers, indicating script-reading.
fluency.calculation: Zero filler words and zero conversational markers resulted in a penalty for script-reading.

relevance.explanation: The presenter added oral explanation beyond the slide text, but the Q&A answers were too brief.
relevance.calculation: Oral explanation beyond slide text was indicated (no PSE triggered), but Q&A responses were minimal.

delivery.explanation: The presenter spoke at an extremely slow pace, far below the TED golden zone.
delivery.calculation: 8 WPM is significantly slower than the 160-190 WPM TED golden zone.

what_i_did_good: [Structure] Clear Slide Titles: Your slides showed clear titles like 'Introduction' and 'Core Data & Market Validation'. These helped organize your talk.
[Fluency] No Filler Words: You did not use any filler words. This kept your speech sounding clean.
[Content Relevance] Beyond Text: You added information beyond what was written on the slides. This gave the audience more understanding.
[Delivery] Simple Language: You used simple words when you said: 'confidence'. This makes it easy for the audience to understand.

areas_for_improvement.issue: [Structure] Weak Message Map: Your headlines for each slide were not explicit in the narration.
areas_for_improvement.example: You said: 'slide 1 narration text here', which did not clearly introduce the slide's purpose or main point.
areas_for_improvement.how_to_fix: Make it a Twitter-style headline (under 140 characters). Say this instead: 'Let's begin by looking at how our solution fixes a big problem for students.'

areas_for_improvement.issue: [Fluency] Script Reading over Dialogue: Your tone sounded like reading a paper rather than a TED-style talk.
areas_for_improvement.example: You literally said: 'confidence' to answer a detailed question, which lacked natural connectors or conversational phrasing.
areas_for_improvement.how_to_fix: Internalize your content and speak like a conversation. Say this instead: 'We measured confidence through [method], and the sample size [explanation].'

areas_for_improvement.issue: [Content Relevance] Lack of Detail: Your answers to questions were very short and did not provide enough detail.
areas_for_improvement.example: When asked for concrete details about the case study, you only said: 'it helps students a lot'.
areas_for_improvement.how_to_fix: Provide specific examples and data to support your claims. Say this instead: 'The case study involved [WHO] from [WHERE] during [WHEN], and it showed [specific outcome].'

areas_for_improvement.issue: [Delivery] TOO SLOW: Your WPM of 8 is far below the TED golden zone of 160-190 WPM.
areas_for_improvement.example: You spoke: 'slide 1 narration text here', which sounded very hesitant and too slow.
areas_for_improvement.how_to_fix: Record yourself and aim for 160-190 WPM. Say this instead: 'Our solution addresses / a critical gap / in the EdTech market.'

what_i_did_good: [Universal] Directness & Logic (55/100): Your response showed strength here. Keep applying this approach.
[Universal] Conversational Resonance (50/100): Good baseline. Focused practice will push this above 75.
[Class Presentation] 5-W Detail (35/100): You attempted the anchor question. Focus on following the scaffold hint exactly next time.

areas_for_improvement.how_to_fix: Add one concrete fact or story. Say this instead: 'For example, in [year], [who] at [place] did [what], and the result was [outcome].' — one specific case beats ten adjectives.

areas_for_improvement.issue: [Class Presentation] The scaffold hint was provided but the 5-W Detail technique was not fully demonstrated.
areas_for_improvement.example: You said: "it helps students a lot…"
areas_for_improvement.how_to_fix: Say this instead: Demonstrate 5-W Detail in your very first sentence — follow the scaffold exactly and practice until it is automatic.

per_question_analysis.question_text: You report a 43% improvement in presentation confidence after only four sessions with a sample of 50 students across three campuses—could you clarify how confidence was measured, and whether this sample size is sufficient to generalize to your target market of 1.8 million international students?
per_question_analysis.what_i_did_good: You said 'confidence', which correctly names the core keyword the question was asking about. That shows you understood what was being asked.
per_question_analysis.areas_for_improvement: Your slide on 'Core Data & Market Validation' has more specific content (User research with 50 university students across 3 campuses. Results: 43% improvement in presentation confidence after 4 sessions. Target ma…) that you did not use in your answer. Naming that specific detail would make your answer much stronger.
per_question_analysis.how_to_fix: Say this instead: 'confidence' — and here is the specific point from my slide on Core Data & Market Validation: User research with 50 university students across 3 campuses. Results: 43% improvement in presentation confidence after 4 sessions. Target market: 1.8 million in…. That is why this matters for my presentation.

per_question_analysis.question_text: Regarding the case study you mentioned, rather than abstract theory, can you specify the concrete details — specifically WHO was involved and WHEN this occurred? (引导：Illustrative Support。不要讲空话，讲讲具体的时间、人物和发生的事情)
per_question_analysis.what_i_did_good: When you said 'it helps students a lot', you gave a real answer instead of avoiding the question, and it stayed on the same topic the question raised.
per_question_analysis.areas_for_improvement: Your slide on 'Core Data & Market Validation' has more specific content (User research with 50 university students across 3 campuses. Results: 43% improvement in presentation confidence after 4 sessions. Target ma…) that you did not use in your answer. Naming that specific detail would make your answer much stronger.
per_question_analysis.how_to_fix: Say this instead: 'it helps students a lot' — and here is the specific point from my slide on Core Data & Market Validation: User research with 50 university students across 3 campuses. Results: 43% improvement in presentation confidence after 4 sessions. Target market: 1.8 million in…. That is why this matters for my presentation.

evaluation.training_plan:
## Training Plan

**Profile:** A professor needs to improve class presentation skills, especially delivery, to explain methodology better.

### Fix First — Delivery (45/100)

1.  **"Explain Your Method" Drill:** Pick one core research method you use. Stand up and explain it to an empty room for 3 minutes. Do not use notes or slides. Focus on speaking clearly and making eye contact with different parts of the room. This helps you sound natural, not like reading, even for complex methodology.
2.  **"Gesture Your Steps" Drill:** Choose a step-by-step part of your methodology. Practice explaining it using only your hands and body. For example, show how data flows or how one step leads to the next. This helps you move your body and look more engaged when explaining technical details.

### Quick Wins This Week

*   When you use notes, only write keywords. Do not write full sentences.
*   Before your next talk, think of two hard questions. Practice answering them out loud with more detail.
*   Record a 2-minute part of your presentation on your phone. Watch it. Find one small thing to make your voice sound more natural.

### 30-Day Schedule

-   **Week 1:** Focus on speaking from memory. Practice explaining one part of your methodology each day without notes. Work on sounding like you are talking, not reading.
-   **Week 2:** Focus on body language. Practice using simple hand gestures to make your explanations of methods clearer. Look at different people in the room.
-   **Week 3:** Focus on answering questions well. Ask a friend to ask you questions about your work. Practice giving answers that are clear and have enough detail.
-   **Week 4:** Focus on connecting everything. Do a full practice run. Try to make each slide's main point very clear in your voice. Be confident in showing your methodology.

qa_bank.question: You report a 43% improvement in presentation confidence after only four sessions with a sample of 50 students across three campuses—could you clarify how confidence was measured, and whether this sample size is sufficient to generalize to your target market of 1.8 million international students?

Regarding the case study you mentioned, rather than abstract theory, can you specify the concrete details — specifically WHO was involved and WHEN this occurred? (引导：Illustrative Support。不要讲空话，讲讲具体的时间、人物和发生的事情)
qa_bank.scaffold_signal: 5-W specificity: does the user name a specific WHO and WHEN with concrete detail?
